learn.title: Solar PV Panels and the Photoelectric Effect
learn.intro: Solar photovoltaic (PV) panels are critical for decarbonizing energy systems by converting sunlight directly into electricity. Understanding the photoelectric effect—the foundation of solar technology—helps explain how clean energy can scale globally. India aims for 500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030, with solar playing a central role.
learn.c1_text: When sunlight hits a solar panel, photons knock electrons loose in silicon cells. Think of it like raindrops hitting a water wheel—each photon energizes an electron to create a flow of electricity. This process requires semiconductors, typically silicon, doped to form p-n junctions for current generation.
learn.c2_title: ⚡ How Solar Cells Convert Light to Electricity
learn.c2_text: Silicon cells are layered with anti-reflective coatings to maximize light absorption. Electrons freed by photons move through conductive metal plates to generate direct current (DC). Inverters convert DC to alternating current (AC) for homes. Efficiency ranges from 15–22% for commercial panels, improving with passivation techniques.
learn.c3_text: India's Production Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme allocates ₹195 billion ($2.5 billion) to boost domestic solar manufacturing. It targets 100 GW annual production capacity by 2025–26. This reduces reliance on imports and aligns with India's National Solar Mission to achieve 280 GW renewable energy by 2030.
learn.stat4: India's PLI scheme supports 50 GW solar manufacturing capacity

learn.title: India's National Green Hydrogen Mission Targets
learn.intro: Green hydrogen, produced using renewable energy, is key to decarbonizing hard-to-abate sectors like steel and shipping. India's National Green Hydrogen Mission aims to make the country a global hub, targeting 5 MMT annual production by 2030. This supports energy security and export potential.
learn.c1_text: The mission targets 5 million metric tons (MMT) of green hydrogen production and 175 GW of renewable energy capacity by 2030. Think of it as a race to the moon—India aims to leapfrog into clean energy leadership. It includes pilot projects, infrastructure development, and R&D funding.
learn.c2_text: India offers viability gap funding (VGF) for green hydrogen projects, covering up to 30% of capital costs. The government also provides interest subvention of 2% on loans. These incentives aim to reduce production costs, currently $3–6/kg, to below $2/kg by 2030.
learn.c3_text: India plans to establish hydrogen valleys in Gujarat, Tamil Nadu, and other states. It targets 10 GW electrolyzer manufacturing capacity by 2030. The mission aligns with India's goal to achieve net-zero emissions by 2070, with green hydrogen replacing fossil fuels in industrial processes.
learn.stat1: India aims for 5 MMT green hydrogen production annually by 2030
learn.stat2: ₹195 billion ($2.5 billion) allocated under PLI for hydrogen tech
learn.stat4: India's target: 175 GW renewable energy for green hydrogen by 2030

learn.title: Green Hydrogen Production via Electrolysis
learn.intro: Water electrolysis splits H2O into hydrogen and oxygen using renewable electricity, producing zero-emission green hydrogen. This process is vital for decarbonizing industries and transportation. IRENA highlights electrolysis as a cornerstone for achieving net-zero goals globally.
learn.c1_text: Electrolysis uses electricity to separate water into hydrogen (at cathode) and oxygen (at anode). Think of it like a battery charging—energy splits molecules instead of storing electrons. It requires 50–55 kWh/kg of hydrogen, with efficiency around 60–80% depending on technology.
learn.c2_text: PEM (Proton Exchange Membrane) electrolyzers use a solid polymer membrane, offering faster response and higher purity. Alkaline electrolyzers use liquid electrolyte, are cheaper, but slower. PEM costs $1,000–1,500/kW, while alkaline is $500–800/kW. Both are scaling rapidly.
learn.c3_text: India's PLI scheme includes ₹195 billion for electrolyzer manufacturing, aiming for 10 GW capacity by 2030. NTPC and Indian Oil Corporation are building 10 MW PEM plants. The country plans to export green hydrogen to Japan and Europe, leveraging low-cost renewables.
learn.stat3: India's target: 10 GW electrolyzer manufacturing by 2030
learn.stat4: Green hydrogen production cost goal: $1.50–2.00/kg by 2030

learn.title: Solar Panel Types Compared: Mono vs Poly vs Thin-Film
learn.intro: Choosing the right solar panel type impacts efficiency, cost, and application. Monocrystalline, polycrystalline, and thin-film technologies each have unique advantages. NREL research guides optimal selection based on climate, space, and budget, crucial for India's diverse geography.
learn.c1_text: Monocrystalline panels use single-crystal silicon, achieving 20–22% efficiency. They’re like luxury sports cars—high performance but costly. Polycrystalline uses multiple crystals, 15–20% efficiency, cheaper but bulkier. Think of them as reliable sedans—good value for large-scale projects.
learn.c2_text: Thin-film panels use layers of cadmium telluride or CIGS, just 1–2 microns thick. Efficiency is 10–13%, but they perform better in high heat and low light. Like bicycles, they’re lightweight and flexible but slower. Ideal for rooftops and portable systems.
learn.c3_text: India's PLI scheme prioritizes monocrystalline and polycrystalline manufacturing. It aims to cut import dependence by 70% by 2025. Gujarat and Tamil Nadu host major solar parks using these technologies. Domestic production supports India’s 280 GW renewable target by 2030.
learn.stat3: India's PLI scheme targets 50 GW annual solar manufacturing

learn.title: Hydrogen Color Codes: Blue vs Green vs Grey
learn.intro: Hydrogen's 'color' denotes its production method and carbon footprint. Green hydrogen, made with renewables, is emission-free. Blue uses natural gas with carbon capture. Grey, from fossil fuels, emits CO2. IEA emphasizes green hydrogen as critical for net-zero goals.
learn.c1_text: Green hydrogen uses renewable energy to split water via electrolysis. Think of it as cooking with a solar stove—zero emissions. It’s the cleanest option but costs $3–6/kg. Production is scaling rapidly in India, Europe, and Australia to meet climate targets.
learn.c2_text: Blue hydrogen uses natural gas steam reforming with carbon capture (80–90% CO2 reduction). Grey hydrogen, from unabated methane, emits 9–12 kg CO2/kg H2. Think of them as gas stoves—convenient but polluting. Blue is a transition fuel; grey is being phased out.
learn.c3_text: India prioritizes green hydrogen under its National Mission, targeting 5 MMT by 2030. It avoids blue hydrogen to align with net-zero goals. The country plans to export green hydrogen, leveraging $1.50–2.00/kg production costs. Policies incentivize renewable-powered electrolysis.
learn.stat2: Blue hydrogen emits 80–90% less CO2 than grey
learn.stat3: India's target: 5 MMT green hydrogen annually by 2030

learn.title: PLI Scheme for Solar Manufacturing in India
learn.intro: India's Production Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme for solar manufacturing aims to reduce import dependence and boost domestic production. With ₹195 billion allocated, it targets 100 GW annual capacity by 2025–26. This supports India's 280 GW renewable energy goal by 2030.
learn.c1_text: The PLI scheme offers incentives based on production capacity and value addition. Manufacturers receive subsidies for setting up polysilicon, wafer, and cell plants. Think of it as seed funding for startups—helping scale production. It prioritizes high-efficiency monocrystalline technologies.
learn.c2_text: The scheme aims for 100 GW annual solar manufacturing capacity by 2025–26. This includes 50 GW polysilicon, 30 GW wafers, and 20 GW cells. It reduces reliance on China, which dominates 80% of global supply. India seeks self-reliance in clean energy tech.
learn.c3_text: PLI supports India's goal to become a solar export hub. It aligns with the National Solar Mission, targeting 280 GW renewables by 2030. States like Gujarat and Tamil Nadu are key manufacturing zones. The scheme could create 150,000 jobs in the sector.
learn.stat1: ₹195 billion ($2.5 billion) allocated under PLI for solar manufacturing
learn.stat2: India targets 100 GW annual solar production by 2025–26
learn.stat3: PLI aims to reduce import dependence by 70% in solar equipment
learn.stat4: India's solar manufacturing employment potential: 150,000 jobs
